EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough of low pressure in the Mississippi Valley tracked northeast through the Ohio Valley. A strong surface low developed in eastern Kentucky. Mixed freezing rain and sleet early on on the evening of the 19th quickly transitioned to a heavy snow overnight. This system was followed by bitter cold and sub-zero wind chills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer in Batesville measured 5 inches of snow. Another observer northeast of Osgood measured 3 inches. Surrounding observations indicated that significant snow and ice accumulations created hazardous conditions across the county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Based on surrounding observations, significant snow and ice accumulations created hazardous conditions across the county, particularly the northwestern half.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Areas of frost occurred during the early morning hours as temperatures dipped into the lower 30's. Agricultural experts reported no significant effects on protected crops or  fruit trees. The only reported damage was some minor cold injury that affected random asparagus spears, but there was nothing widespread.  A ridge of high pressure extending from the Gulf coast northward to the lower Ohio and mid Mississippi Valleys provided clear skies and light winds. These conditions were conducive for frost.

A hard freeze was observed across southwest Indiana. Low temperatures were in the middle 20's in most areas. Agricultural experts reported some damage to peach buds, but not enough to ruin the peach crop. More than one peach grower reported highly variable peach bud survival, ranging from some trees with no bud survival to other trees with no apparent cold injury. Early crops such as cucumbers were generally unaffected if preventive measures were taken against freeze damage. High pressure from the Ozark Mountains through the Ohio Valley created ideal conditions for frost and freezing temperatures. Winds were light with clear skies.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance produced occasional showers and embedded thunderstorms during the morning hours, leading to localized flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High water flooded St Marys Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance produced occasional showers and embedded thunderstorms during the morning hours, leading to localized flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A car was stranded in high water on Huntersville Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours in an unstable air mass that was in place across the Ohio Valley.
An unstable, but poorly sheared air mass allowed storms to form, but quickly collapse. One such collapse caused damage in a few spots across Cass county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management officials reported a 18 inch diameter tree was blown down at the intersection of Burlington Ave and Dewey St.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable, but poorly sheared air mass allowed storms to form, but quickly collapse. One such collapse caused damage in a few spots across Cass county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management officials reported two trees were blown down on Burlington Ave near Third St. One tree was around 18 inches in diameter, with the other slightly smaller.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed, but struggled to organize with mainly 30 to 50 mph wind gusts. Boundary interactions allowed for 2 storms to briefly produce wet microbursts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local media reported a tree was blown down onto power lines.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported a large tree was blown down along with construction signs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unstable but poorly sheared environment was in place with scattered thunderstorms developing during the late afternoon hours. One thunderstorm produced a marginally severe wind gust but no damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station recorded a 59 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of storms moved across northern Indiana. One thunderstorm produced near severe hail in White county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail stones varied from blueberry and coco puff size to roughly nickel size.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed, but struggled to organize with mainly 30 to 50 mph wind gusts. Boundary interactions allowed for 2 storms to briefly produce wet microbursts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 65 year old male and a friend were heading for shore getting away from a thunderstorms when lightning struck the 65 year old. CPR was started but they were unable to save him. The other passenger was not harmed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains toward the end of December sent the Ohio River above flood stage from December into the first part of January. The flooding was minor, consisting mostly of flooded bottomland fields, woodlands, and public park and recreation areas. Some lesser-traveled roads in the bottomlands were flooded.

Outflow boundaries from convection across central Lower Michigan and subtle shortwaves passing through the region caused thunderstorms to develop. High CAPE values but low shear allowed the storms to form quickly but struggle to organize. Eventually, greater DCAPE values arrive with one of the waves and caused more extensive wind damage in a few locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local media reported multiple trees were blown down or uprooted on CR 500 N.

An upper level system produced a period of accumulating snow on Saturday January 12th. Snowfall amounts ranged between 3 and 5 inches north of US 30 to 4 to 8 inches south of US 30.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow developed during the morning hours of January 12th and continued into the afternoon and early evening. Snow amounts of 4 to 6 inches were common across the county. There were reports of minor accidents and slide-offs across the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening low pressure system through the Ohio Valley produced a swath of accumulating snow and strong winds across the Lower Great Lakes region on Saturday, January 19th. Snow totals of 4 to 8 inches and wind gusts to near 40 mph created difficult travel conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to at times heavy snow developed during the morning hours of January 19th and continued through the rest of the day. Winds really picked up during the day, gusting between 30 and 40 mph during the afternoon and early evening hours. Snow accumulations across the county generally ranged between 4 and 6 inches, with significant blowing and drifting reported due to the strong winds. Hudson received 5.8 of total snow accumulation. Slide-offs and accidents were reported across the region. A few secondary roads were impassable due to the significant drifting.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Alberta Clipper brought heavy snow and gusty winds to Lower Michigan January 28th into early January 29th. Snow accumulations generally ranged between 3 and 8 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow overspread around daybreak on January 28th and continued through early January 29th. Some freezing drizzle did mix in during the late afternoon and evening hours. Total snow accumulations across the county ranged between 5 and 8 inches. La Porte received 6.5 inches of snow. Gusty winds to near 30 mph led to considerable blowing and drifting snow which resulted in limited visibility and difficult travel. There were numerous school delays and cancellations, along with reports of accidents and slide-offs across the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening low pressure system through the Ohio Valley produced a swath of accumulating snow and strong winds across the Lower Great Lakes region on Saturday, January 19th. Snow totals of 4 to 8 inches and wind gusts to near 40 mph created difficult travel conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to at times heavy snow developed during the morning hours of January 19th and continued through the rest of the day. Winds really picked up during the day, gusting between 35 and 45 mph during the afternoon and early evening hours. Snow accumulations across the county generally ranged between 5 and 7 inches, with significant blowing and drifting reported due to the strong winds. Bluffton received 6.5 of total snow accumulation. Slide-offs and accidents were reported across the region. A few secondary roads were impassable due to the significant drifting.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dangerous arctic air mass overspread northern Indiana late on January 29th through the morning hours of January 31st. Wind chills between 30 and 50 degrees below zero were common.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusty west winds ushered in a bitterly cold arctic air mass late on January 29th through the morning hours of January 31st. The coldest of the air and strongest winds occurred during the morning hours of January 30th when the wind chill value at Tri-Lakes dropped to 35 degrees below zero. Wind chills through the event generally ranged between 25 and 50 degrees below zero. The coldest air temperature recorded at Tri-Lakes was 16 degrees below zero on January 31st. Many businesses and schools were closed due to this record breaking shot of dangerous cold air.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread snow, mixed at times with sleet, spread across southwest Indiana during the early evening hours on January 11th. This was in response to surface low pressure that developed across the Texas and Oklahoma region. This low then moved east-northeastward across the Missouri Bootheel by early evening on the 12th. Warmer temperatures at the surface and aloft gradually changed the precipitation to rain from south to north. The changeover occurred during the pre-dawn hours of the 12th along the Ohio River, then progressed north to the White River by late morning. Sleet was not widespread enough to significantly lower snowfall amounts. The heaviest snowfall amounts from this system were around four inches from Evansville west to the Wabash River. Amounts were mainly two to three inches north of Interstate 64 and east of Evansville. There were significant travel impacts during the evening hours and into the overnight hours, as snow-covered roads led to numerous accidents. The snow fell at  the rate of nearly an inch per hour during part of the evening. Three inches was measured in four hours along the Posey-Vanderburgh County line.

Extremely cold air moved across northwest Indiana on January 30th and 31st.  Low temperatures on January 30th included -20 at Gary; -19 at Valparaiso and -18 at Rensselaer.  The lowest observed wind chills included -51 at Gary; -47 at Rensselaer and at Valparaiso.

This likely played a part in a rollover crash on I-69 in Huntington County Saturday morning. This accident occurred around 7:24 am in the morning killing a 29 year old man.

An area of low pressure moved through Kentucky bringing a wintry mix of snow, sleet, and freezing rain to central Indiana on January 19th. Warm air above the ground caused more freezing rain to fall than expected in some areas, cutting down snow totals. An upper level component to the storm brought a band of heavy snow across mainly southern sections of the area later in the afternoon into the evening. Winds gusting to over 40 mph later in the day brought white-out conditions to parts of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Accumulating ice had many roads impassable. Many vehicles were abandoned.  Spotty power outages were also observed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Freezing drizzle and freezing rain developed during the afternoon and early evening hours of January 22nd creating slick roads. Ice accretions of a glaze to one tenth of an inch were common.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain and drizzle developed mid-afternoon on January 22nd and continued into the evening. Temperatures did warm above freezing into the morning of January 23rd while the rain continued, though sub-freezing road temperatures kept most secondary roads and sidewalks slick into January 23rd. There were numerous reports of slide-offs and accidents across the region. Many schools and businesses across the region were closed due to these icy road conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Alberta Clipper brought heavy snow and gusty winds to Lower Michigan January 28th into early January 29th. Snow accumulations generally ranged between 3 and 8 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow overspread around daybreak on January 28th and continued through early January 29th. Some freezing drizzle did mix in during the late afternoon and evening hours. Total snow accumulations across the county ranged between 3 and 5 inches. This in tandem with 30 mph wind gusts created difficult travel conditions. There were numerous school delays and cancellations, along with reports of accidents and slide-offs across the region. An accident between a police officer and a SUV resulted in 2 fatalities (the police officer and his wife) Monday night.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow developed across portions of central Indiana ahead of an approaching area of low pressure. Plentiful moisture combined with strong isentropic lift produced a widespread area of heavy snow. This winter system led to the first January winter storm warning since 2014. Snow fell starting late Friday night and continued through Saturday night.  During the afternoon on Saturday, temperatures slowly climbed above freezing across south central Indiana which led to lower amounts of snow. Snowfall totals ranged from 4 inches to as high as 9 inches in Owen County. The 6.9inches of snow at Indianapolis tied the snowfall record for the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 6.5 inches was observed one mile north of Flora in Carroll County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Freezing drizzle and freezing rain developed during the afternoon and early evening hours of January 22nd creating slick roads. Ice accretions of a glaze to one tenth of an inch were common.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain and drizzle developed mid-afternoon on January 22nd and continued into the evening. Temperatures did warm above freezing into the morning of January 23rd while the rain continued, though sub-freezing road temperatures kept most secondary roads and sidewalks slick into January 23rd. There were numerous reports of slide-offs and accidents across the region. Many schools and businesses across the region were closed due to these icy road conditions. An aircraft operating a regional flight out of the Fort Wayne International Airport slide while taxiing for takeoff and came to a stop in a snow bank Tuesday evening (January 22). There no were no injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dangerous arctic air mass overspread northern Indiana late on January 29th through the morning hours of January 31st. Wind chills between 30 and 50 degrees below zero were common.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusty west winds ushered in a bitterly cold arctic air mass late on January 29th through the morning hours of January 31st. The coldest of the air and strongest winds occurred during the morning hours of January 30th when the wind chill value near La Porte dropped to 46 degrees below zero. Wind chills through the event generally ranged between 25 and 50 degrees below zero. The coldest air temperature recorded at La Porte was 25 degrees below zero on January 31st. Many businesses and schools were closed on both January 30th and January 31st due to this record breaking shot of dangerous cold air. Gusty winds up to 40 mph also led to areas of blowing and drifting snow that helped wreak havoc on area roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deepening low pressure center tracked from eastern Arkansas across middle Tennessee during the day, then into southeast Kentucky on the evening of the 19th. As colder air quickly pushed southward, rain changed to snow during the late afternoon and early evening hours. Along and north of I-64, travel impacts were very high due to the combination of high snowfall rates (1 to 2 inches per hour), strong wind gusts of 30 to 40 mph, and rapidly falling temperatures. Roads quickly became snow and ice covered, and visibility was frequently around one-quarter mile for a few hours. Near blizzard conditions were experienced for a few hours north of I-64. There were numerous accidents, and travel became difficult. The heaviest snowfall totals were observed north of I-64 in Gibson and Pike Counties, where 4 to 8 inches fell. The lowest amounts were observed east of Evansville in Spencer and southern Warrick Counties, where 1 to 2 inches fell. Measuring the snowfall accurately was a challenge due to the strong winds throughout the late afternoon and evening hours. Convective elements, including thundersnow, produced snowfall rates from one to two inches per hour north of I-64. Temperatures fell into the mid 20's by the end of the snowstorm, producing wind chills from 10 to 15.

An upper level trough of low pressure in the Mississippi Valley tracked northeast through the Ohio Valley. A strong surface low developed in eastern Kentucky. Mixed freezing rain and sleet early on on the evening of the 19th quickly transitioned to a heavy snow overnight. This system was followed by bitter cold and sub-zero wind chills.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Based on surrounding observations, significant snowfall amounts over 4 inches were estimated to have fallen across the county. This was combined with freezing rain creating additional driving hazards.

More heavy rain impacted the region shortly after the middle part of the month, once again causing rivers to rise above flood stage by the end of January. The flooding was minor, consisting mostly of flooded bottomland fields, woodlands, and public park and recreation areas. Some lesser-traveled roads in the bottomlands were flooded. The flooding continued into February on the Ohio, White, and Wabash Rivers.

Strong west to northwest winds overspread southwest Indiana behind a cold front. The cold front swept eastward beneath a low pressure center over the Great Lakes region. A peak wind gust to 41 mph was measured at the Evansville airport.

An area of low pressure moved through Kentucky bringing a wintry mix of snow, sleet, and freezing rain to central Indiana on January 19th. Warm air above the ground caused more freezing rain to fall than expected in some areas, cutting down snow totals. An upper level component to the storm brought a band of heavy snow across mainly southern sections of the area later in the afternoon into the evening. Winds gusting to over 40 mph later in the day brought white-out conditions to parts of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of near 6 inches fell just northwest of Peoga.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain from a storm system that impacted central Indiana from the evening of the 22nd through the evening of the 23rd. Flooding occurred on the East Fork White River, which spilled over onto a road in Daviess County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported on the Daviess and Pike County Line on State Road 257, adjacent to the East Fork White River due to heavy rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large low pressure system advanced east behind a cold front. Ahead of the front, widespread rain provided a soaking rain for southern Indiana and central Kentucky. Crashing temperatures during the evening changed precipitation over to sleet, then quickly to snow. There was 1 to 3 inches of snow over much of southern Indiana and central Kentucky. Orange County saw up to 7 inches of snow before snow showers moved to the east during the early morning hours of the 20th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Across Orange County snow depths ranged from 4 inches, in West Baden, to 7 inches, a mile north of Prospect.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread snow, mixed at times with sleet, spread across southwest Indiana during the early evening hours on January 11th. This was in response to surface low pressure that developed across the Texas and Oklahoma region. This low then moved east-northeastward across the Missouri Bootheel by early evening on the 12th. Warmer temperatures at the surface and aloft gradually changed the precipitation to rain from south to north. The changeover occurred during the pre-dawn hours of the 12th along the Ohio River, then progressed north to the White River by late morning. Sleet was not widespread enough to significantly lower snowfall amounts. The heaviest snowfall amounts from this system were around four inches from Evansville west to the Wabash River. Amounts were mainly two to three inches north of Interstate 64 and east of Evansville. There were significant travel impacts during the evening hours and into the overnight hours, as snow-covered roads led to numerous accidents. The snow fell at  the rate of nearly an inch per hour during part of the evening. Three inches was measured in four hours along the Posey-Vanderburgh County line.

Extremely cold air moved across northwest Indiana on January 30th and 31st.  Low temperatures on January 30th included -20 at Gary; -19 at Valparaiso and -18 at Rensselaer.  The lowest observed wind chills included -51 at Gary; -47 at Rensselaer and at Valparaiso.

An intense low pressure system developed across the western Great Lakes on Sunday, February 24th. Widespread 30 to 40 mph prevailing west winds occurred with gusts of 45 to 68 mph. These high winds were of long duration (approximately 13-16 hours) and this was considered unprecedented based on local climatology. Numerous reports of downed large trees, large tree limbs, and utility poles were received along with many power outages across the entire area. The highest wind gust of the event was at KAOH with a 68 mph gust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Long duration sustained winds of 35 to 45 mph with gusts 50 to 55 mph occurred across the county, resulting in trees, tree limbs and power poles suffering damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense low pressure system developed across the western Great Lakes on Sunday, February 24th. Widespread 30 to 40 mph prevailing west winds occurred with gusts of 45 to 68 mph. These high winds were of long duration (approximately 13-16 hours) and this was considered unprecedented based on local climatology. The duration of this event was really unprecedented in terms of the number of hours with wind gusts measured at or above 58 mph (50kts) at Fort Wayne. There were 16 transmitted observations on the 24th with a 50+ knot wind report.

Snow continued into the evening along the I-70 corridor with generally 2 to 3 inches of accumulation. Significantly less snow occurred further south.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snowy roads and speed of travel played a role in a car crash that killed a father and his two daughters in Noble County on February 11th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Police say 50-year-old JJ Reyes was driving the car with his two daughters, 15-year-old Zulia Reyes and 13-year-old Valeria Reyes. He approached a curve on the snowy road and lost control of the vehicle. Police say it appears his speed was too fast for the conditions. The car slid off the road and went into a ditch before sliding into the Elkhart River near County Road 700 West and Ball Road in Ligonier. All 3 were killed in the accident around 10:00 am February 11th. Roads were snow covered and slick in this area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved through the Great Lakes region, sparking a brief round of accumulating snowfall early in the morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer north of Bright measured an inch of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved along a front across southern parts of central Indiana during the late afternoon and evening of April 25th. The system brought two tornadoes, damaging winds, and heavy rain which caused flooding. The system had shear and instability to work with to produce the tornadoes. In addition, plenty of moisture was available, which lead to the heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This EF1 tornado, with winds estimated at up to 95 mph, snapped and uprooted several trees along County Road 1000 North, west of County Road 740 East. This tornado occurred 12 miles northeast of North Vernon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved through the Great Lakes region, sparking a brief round of accumulating snowfall early in the morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report from Brookville showed that an inch of snow fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain accumulated to near a quarter of an inch during the morning of February 12th as low pressure lifted north toward the Lower Great Lakes region. This created difficult travel, along with scattered power outages due to falling trees and tree limbs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain, at times mixed with sleet, accumulated up to 0.30 inches in spots on February 12th. Numerous slide-offs and minor accidents occurred across the county. Many schools and some businesses were closed on February 12th due to the icy conditions. Fallen tree branches were reported across the region, with many onto roads and power lines creating scattered power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wintry mix of snow, freezing drizzle and sleet had a significant impact on the February 20th morning commute. Light snow and ice accumulations were common across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow, freezing drizzle and sleet created icy roads and sidewalks during the morning hours of February 20th. Numerous schools and some businesses were closed. There were also reports of slide-offs and accidents across the region.

In addition, plenty of moisture was available, which lead to the heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF1 tornado, with maximum winds estimated at up to 100 mph, destroyed a garage at a home on County Road 25 East. Debris strewn to the north and northeast and across the road. Several concrete anchors ripped out from the garage foundation. Trailer flipped and rolled 15 feet. Two cars pushed short distances, one with window blown out.  Air conditioner moved on the west side of the adjacent house. Glass patio table tossed back south and damaged. Multiple trees and limbs twisted and downed. Second home on County Road 400 South sustained substantial loss of roofing including removal of all three dormers. Underside of roof of north facing front porch blown out with insulation debris caked on the north and east sides of the house. Wicker chair from front porch tossed to the left with two legs impaled into the windshield of a nearby SUV. This tornado occurred 4 miles south of North Vernon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system brought rain, wind, and some thunderstorms to central Indiana on the evening of March 9th into March 10th. A few houses were damaged in the Eaton area from thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three homes sustained damage with one having the roof blown off due to damaging thunderstorm wind gusts. Some vehicles also sustained damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong to severe thunderstorms moved across northwest Indiana during the afternoon of March 14th.  Strong non-thunderstorm winds developed in the afternoon, after the storms ended.  Winds gusted to 62 mph at Valparaiso Airport and to 58 mph at Gary Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Through pictures and other data provided by the Lake County, Indiana Emergency Management Agency, as well as trained storm spotters, a short lived EF-0 tornado was confirmed on the outskirts of the community of Lowell.  The actual tornado may have very briefly lifted once or twice during its 2 to 3 minute duration, per reports and damage.  The damage was limited mostly to trees, with portions of a few large trees downed including one on a home.  There was also some shingle damage on another home, multiple fences damaged and one utility pole snapped and others damaged.

A very large high in the upper levels of the atmosphere was sprawled across the southern two-thirds of the country for several days. This high brought four days of oppressive heat and humidity across southwest Indiana. Afternoon heat indices peaked between 100 and 104 degrees each day from the 18th to the 21st. The heat index at Evansville peaked at 100 degrees on the 18th, and 102 to 103 from the 19th to the 21st. Just prior to this heat wave, the remnants of Tropical Storm Barry produced a widespread soaking rain that set the stage for very high humidity levels. Dew points were in the mid to upper 70's. Actual high temperatures were mostly in the lower 90's.

Scattered thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front on the morning and early afternoon of the 14th, producing isolated wind damage and large hail. Locally heavy rain resulted in isolated flooding. Through most of the day on the 14th, strong south gradient winds associated with the strong low pressure system gusted up to 55 mph at times. The low pressure center tracked northeast across the Plains to the Great Lakes region. The peak measured non-thunderstorm wind gust at the Evansville airport was 58 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A utility pole was down in a neighborhood southeast of downtown Evansville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heat indices peaked between 105 and 110 degrees during the afternoon hours. A combination of temperatures in the mid 90's and dew points in the mid 70's produced these hazardous heat indices. A southwest wind flow of hot and humid air ahead of a cold front was responsible for this first potentially dangerous heat event of the season.

Widespread minor to moderate river flooding across the region gradually subsided in early March. February was an above average month when it came to precipitation and flooding. The central and southern portions of Indiana received 150 to more than 200 percent of normal precipitation. February precipitation ranged from 3 to over 6 inches in portions of southern Indiana. Evansville experienced its second wettest February on record going back to 1897. The monthly rainfall total there was 7.34 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding continued along the Ohio River from February through the first few days of March. About a dozen county roads in the floodplain were flooded, and some were impassable. They were mostly county roads flooded near the river, outside the Evansville city limits. Most of the flooded roads were south of Interstate 69 in the southeast part of the county and in lowland areas in the southwest part of the county. Basements along the riverfront began to fill. Backwater flooding of Pigeon Creek inundated low-lying fields and woodlands. The levee authority operated several pumping stations and closed the flood gates downtown. Property damage estimates are contained in the February report.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved into the forecast area bringing plentiful moisture and produced thunderstorms and waves of moderate to heavy rain to central Indiana around February 7th. The storms produced a tornado in a strong shear and low instability environment. Over 4 inches of rain fell in a couple of days in some areas. The heavy rain produced flash flooding, and extensive flooding developed along rivers and streams.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-0 tornado, with max wind speeds of 83 mph, damaged some outbuildings near the town of Bandmill in Knox County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure center moved northeast from Kansas to northern Illinois. Strong southwest winds occurred in advance of a cold front that trailed south from the low. The strongest winds occurred in the afternoon and evening hours. The peak measured wind gust at the Evansville airport was 46 mph.

Locally heavy rain resulted in isolated flooding. Through most of the day on the 14th, strong south gradient winds associated with the strong low pressure system gusted up to 55 mph at times. The low pressure center tracked northeast across the Plains to the Great Lakes region. The peak measured non-thunderstorm wind gust at the Evansville airport was 58 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large tree limbs were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moved into the forecast area bringing plentiful moisture and produced thunderstorms and waves of moderate to heavy rain to central Indiana around February 7th. The storms produced a tornado in a strong shear and low instability environment. Over 4 inches of rain fell in a couple of days in some areas. The heavy rain produced flash flooding, and extensive flooding developed along rivers and streams.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Interstate 74 was flooded in multiple parts of the county due to flash flooding from heavy rainfall. Multiple other county roads were closed due to high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread minor to moderate river flooding across the region gradually subsided in early March. February was an above average month when it came to precipitation and flooding. The central and southern portions of Indiana received 150 to more than 200 percent of normal precipitation. February precipitation ranged from 3 to over 6 inches in portions of southern Indiana. Evansville experienced its second wettest February on record going back to 1897. The monthly rainfall total there was 7.34 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding continued along the Ohio River from February into the first week of March. Low-lying fields and woodlands were underwater.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding continued along the Ohio River from February. At the Mt. Vernon river gage, the river crested at 46.4 feet on February 19th. Large portions of agricultural Point Township in Posey County were flooded. Farmland and some roads were flooded in places southwest and west of Mt. Vernon. Spring planting was not yet underway, so crop losses were not major.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread minor to moderate river flooding across the region gradually subsided in early March. February was an above average month when it came to precipitation and flooding. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding on the Wabash River ended during the first few days of March. At the New Harmony, IN river gage, the river crested at 20.41 feet on February 16th. Flood stage is 15 feet. Considerable flooding of low ground occurred, but most of the low ground was dormant agricultural fields. Some county roads were flooded. Water encroached on parts of northern New Harmony, and Harmonie State Park was closed. Some stripper oil wells were flooded along the river from New Harmony to Mt. Vernon Indiana.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread minor to moderate river flooding across the region gradually subsided in early March. February was an above average month when it came to precipitation and flooding. The central and southern portions of Indiana received 150 to more than 200 percent of normal precipitation. February precipitation ranged from 3 to over 6 inches in portions of southern Indiana. Evansville experienced its second wettest February on record going back to 1897. The monthly rainfall total there was 7.34 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding continued on the White River from February into the first few days of March. At the Hazleton river gage, the river crested at 26.67 feet on Feb. 15th. Flood stage is 16 feet. Flood gates were installed at Hazleton. Water was nearly two feet deep in the west end of Hazleton, where water pumps were installed. People in cabins on the riverward side of the levee generally relocated. Many local roads were completely impassable. Oil fields were inaccessible.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread minor to moderate river flooding across the region gradually subsided in early March. February was an above average month when it came to precipitation and flooding. The central and southern portions of Indiana received 150 to more than 200 percent of normal precipitation. February precipitation ranged from 3 to over 6 inches in portions of southern Indiana. Evansville experienced its second wettest February on record going back to 1897. The monthly rainfall total there was 7.34 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding continued on the White River from February into the first two days of March. At the Petersburg river gage, the river crested at 25.59 feet on Feb. 14th. Flood stage is 16 feet. River cabins in the bottomlands were evacuated. Livestock were moved. Water was over State Route 257. Numerous county roads were flooded, some by several feet of water. Several small oil fields are inaccessible. Considerable amounts of farmland were flooded, but planting of crops was not underway yet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rivers began rising again by mid-month as a pair of strong low pressure systems lifted northeast from the Plains to the Great Lakes region. The first system produced widespread heavy rain on the 9th, followed by another heavy rainfall event on the 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding redeveloped on the Ohio River. Areas of low-lying farm fields and woodlands were underwater. A few rural county roads were affected. Planting season was not yet underway for most crops.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rivers began rising again by mid-month as a pair of strong low pressure systems lifted northeast from the Plains to the Great Lakes region. The first system produced widespread heavy rain on the 9th, followed by another heavy rainfall event on the 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding redeveloped on the Wabash River. Low-lying woods and fields were underwater. A few local river roads were closed by high water. All oil field production ceased with the exception of pumping units on substructures, which were accessible by boat only. Agricultural losses were minor since crop season was not in progress.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rivers began rising again by mid-month as a pair of strong low pressure systems lifted northeast from the Plains to the Great Lakes region. The first system produced widespread heavy rain on the 9th, followed by another heavy rainfall event on the 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding redeveloped on the Wabash River. Low-lying woods and fields and some rural county roads were under water. Crop damage was minimal since planting season was not underway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rivers began rising again by mid-month as a pair of strong low pressure systems lifted northeast from the Plains to the Great Lakes region. The first system produced widespread heavy rain on the 9th, followed by another heavy rainfall event on the 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding redeveloped on the White River. Most low bottomlands were flooded. Some river cabins were affected. Several rural roads along the river outside of levee protection were flooded. Oil fields were flooded. Flood gates were installed at Hazleton. Flood waters covered the Hazleton softball field.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heat indices peaked between 105 and 110 degrees during the afternoon hours. A combination of temperatures in the mid 90's and dew points in the mid 70's produced these hazardous heat indices. A southwest wind flow of hot and humid air ahead of a cold front was responsible for this first potentially dangerous heat event of the season.

The most extensive damage was to a pole barn, which was lifted and destroyed and a grain silo roof which was lifted and blown in an opposite direction and destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first of several rounds of non severe thunderstorms moved through northern Indiana. Lightning from one of these storms struck a home in Knox, causing a fire which destroyed the home.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck a home in the city of Knox during a thunderstorm Tuesday evening. Local media did not provide an exact time, but appeared to be early evening so the time has been estimated. The home was a total loss. No one was injured in the fire. Damage is estimated at $75,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first week of April was dominated by northwest flow aloft and several weak impulses moving southeasterly through the flow. There were a couple rounds of showers and even a few isolated thunderstorms that helped push the Wabash River above flood stage near New Harmony.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred on the Wabash River. Low-lying woods and fields were inundated, along with a couple of county roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low over the northern Plains moved northeast toward the Great Lakes throughout the day, with an associated surface cold front draping south from the system. Ahead of the front a strong low level jet caused southern Indiana and central Kentucky to be placed under a Wind Advisory earlier in the day. As the front approached from the west, severe weather become more of an issue. Widespread wind damage was reported across southern Indiana, including a tornado that touched down in Washington County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS storm survey team found sporadic twisting tree damage between Mount Carmel and McKinley towards the White River. The most significant damage was east of McKinley with several healthy trees snapped and twisted. Most of the trees were snapped halfway up indicative of a skipping weak tornado. On Christmas Tree Road a 30 x 30 barn had significant damage south of the main tree damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Wabash, White, and portions of the Ohio River rose above flood stage during the middle to late part of the month. The flooding was minor, consisting mainly of inundated fields and woodlands in the flood plain. Some county river roads were flooded. Access to  hunting cabins was blocked. Oil fields in the flood plain were inaccessible. After a rather dry start to April, the weather pattern turned wetter by mid-month. A series of three heavy rainfall events caused river levels to rise. The first round of heavy rain occurred from the 11th to the 14th, followed by a second round from the 18th to the 20th, and another round from the 23rd to the 25th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms were lifting northeast across central and eastern portions of central Indiana during the afternoon of April 14th. One strong thunderstorm, the most intense to affect central Indiana that afternoon, pushed northeast across Decatur and southeast Rush Counties. This storm produced a damaging wind and large hail report.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roof was blown off of a trailer due to damaging thunderstorm wind gusts. A barn in bad shape was also destroyed and part of a roof was blown off of 4 barns. Siding was pulled off of a house. Trees, one onto a car, and power lines were also reported down south and southwest of Greensburg. No injuries were reported and there were multiple reports of a funnel. Damage survey determined the damage was caused by strong straight-line wind gusts.

The White and Wabash Rivers remained above flood stage from May into early June. The flooding on both rivers was minor. The region remained stuck in a long-term wet pattern that continued through the spring and into early summer. Most locations were 1 to 2 inches above normal for the month of June. Some locations saw as much as 3 to 5 inches more rainfall than normal in June. At Evansville, Indiana, it was the 6th wettest June on record with a total of 7.53 inches.

A large thunderstorm complex tracked slowly east across southwest Indiana during the morning. These storms produced heavy rainfall and isolated flooding. A deep southwest wind flow through the depth of the atmosphere brought abundant moisture for storms. Precipitable water values were near 1.5 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was reported flowing across Interstate 64 in a few places.

Light freezing rain produced a light glaze of ice on area roadways. Accumulations of ice were just a few hundredths of an inch. The ice had a major impact on the early morning commute. Numerous accidents were reported. Schools were closed in most areas, including Vanderburgh, Warrick, Spencer, Pike, and Gibson Counties. The U.S. Highway 41 twin bridges over the Ohio River were closed due to the ice. Salt trucks were unable to treat the bridge due to a traffic snarl caused by an accident. The freezing rain was caused by a warm front that extended from west to east across the lower Ohio Valley.

Icy roads from freezing rain and light snow likely played a part in a car crash that killed 2 people and injured another Sunday afternoon, February 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two people died and another was hurt in an accident where two vehicles slid on ice and collided on Stellhorn Street Sunday afternoon, February 17th. Roads were icy in spots due to sub-freezing temperatures and light freezing drizzle.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved through the Great Lakes region, sparking a brief round of accumulating snowfall early in the morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observers in Batesville and northeast of Osgood both measured an inch of snow. The cooperative observer in Napoleon also measured an inch of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding continued from late January into the first week of February on the Ohio, Wabash, and White Rivers. The flooding was minor, consisting mostly of flooded bottomland fields, woodlands, and public park and recreation areas. Some lesser-traveled roads in the bottomlands were flooded. Heavy rain impacted the region shortly after the middle part of January, causing this particular flood wave.

A low pressure system strengthened as it moved northeast into the Great Lakes region.  Behind a trailing cold front, west winds gusted from 40 to 50 mph late on the night of the 23rd into the early morning of the 24th. The peak wind gust at the Evansville airport was 47 mph. Isolated power outages were reported, affecting several hundred customers.

A line of thunderstorms developed early in the morning from western Arkansas into south central Missouri ahead of a strong cold front. This line of storms intensified as it moved east-northeast across the mid Mississippi Valley later in the morning. Some severe weather continued through the midday hours as the line progressed up the lower Ohio Valley. This line of storms was the final round of heavy rain, which capped a very wet couple days that resulted in storm totals of 2 to 4 inches. Isolated higher amounts up to near 5 inches were reported. Isolated flooding was reported. Although instability was weak, very strong winds aloft facilitated isolated severe storms and a tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A creek was overflowing its banks and flooding a side road several miles west of Evansville.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service damage survey determined that damage in Oakland City was caused by a tornado with a peak intensity rating of EF-1.  Maximum winds were estimated near 110 mph. A large metal storage building lost most of its roof at a golf course. Several apartment buildings received significant roof damage, with many shingles removed. A self storage building near the apartments had partial roof damage. Additional homes had minor shingle damage. Several large trees were snapped and knocked over. Power poles were snapped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: February ended up being an above average month when it came to precipitation and flooding. The central and southern portions of Indiana ended the month with 150 to more than 200 percent of normal precipitation. Precipitation ranged from 3 to over 6 inches in portions of southern Indiana. Evansville experienced its second wettest February on record going back to 1897. The monthly rainfall total there was 7.34 inches. Widespread minor to moderate river flooding occurred across the region starting around the 8th and continuing through much of the month.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding occurred on the Wabash River. At the Mt. Carmel river gage, the river crested at 28.38 feet on the 15th. Flood stage is 19 feet. There was considerable inundation of low-lying woodlands and fields. Several local roads were flooded, some by several feet of water. Extensive agricultural flooding was in progress in areas that are not protected by levees, however spring planting season had not begun.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain developed during the afternoon of February 11th and continued into the morning of February 12th causing significant ice accumulations across far northwest Indiana.  Valparaiso Airport (VPZ) measured a storm total of 0.41 inches of ice accumulation.

This was sufficient to cause slippery road conditions and some accidents.

February 5th and 6th saw a low pressure system with embedded cold front stall near the Ohio River Valley. It finally pushed to the southeast through central Kentucky on the 7th. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On February 19, 2019, a broad upper trough dipped south to the Gulf of Mexico and carried abundant amounts of moisture towards the Ohio Valley. Once the moisture transportation was underway, isentropic lift caused 1.5 to 3 of rainfall along the Mississippi and Ohio River Valleys. The higher amounts went as far north as south central Indiana. That area saw some flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a road closed on the west side of Jasper due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As strong solar heating destabilized the atmosphere, numerous clusters of intense thunderstorms blossomed from southeast Missouri to southwest Indiana. The main impact associated with these storms was flash flooding. Several locations picked up torrential rainfall amounts over a short time period. Over four inches of rain occurred in Pike County. Damaging winds up to around 60 mph occurred with several of the storms as well. The environment was characterized by moderate to strong instability and moderate wind shear. Effective bulk wind shear values of 25 to 40 knots were observed. The storms developed east of a weak low pressure center over Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds gusted to near 60 mph in spots in the Evansville metro area. Four miles north-northwest of downtown Evansville, a tree fell down on power lines. Several miles east-southeast of Evansville, a trained spotter estimated wind gusts from 50 to 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the heat of the day across southern Illinois into southwest Indiana ahead of a disturbance in the upper levels of the atmosphere approaching from southwest Missouri. A surface frontal boundary was near and just north of Interstate 64. The combination of these features resulted in thunderstorms with locally heavy rain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Southeast parts of Pike County experienced some flooding. Water was over county roads, and severe ponding occurred on Highway 64. Cup Creek was at bankful. A Cocorahs observer measured 0.80 inches in 30 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clusters of thunderstorms repeatedly moved over the same areas, causing isolated minor flooding problems. The storms increased in response to daytime heating of a very moist air mass. A weak low pressure system lifted slowly up the Tennessee River Valley, bringing tropical moisture for locally heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was over the road near the city limits of Rockport. The highway department put up signs to divert traffic from the flooded area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous clusters of thunderstorms developed in southern Illinois and southwest Indiana along an outflow boundary from earlier storms, and ahead of a cold front over northern Indiana. Strong solar heating contributed to a very unstable atmosphere, with mixed-layer capes from 3000 to 3500. Weak wind shear limited storm organization, but strong buoyancy supported intense storms capable of damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and power lines were down in Princeton. Approximately 1000 customers were without power in the Patoka and Hazleton areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed on a stalled frontal boundary draped across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado first touched down on the 1700 block of North U.S. Route 27, where a swirl was observed in a field just to the southwest of a well-constructed, 110 ft by 105 ft metal farm storage building. Several 4 X 4`s were snapped and the building was completely destroyed. Metal debris from this building was identified over 2 miles to the northeast. Wood debris from the barn was located from south to north in a semicircle approximately two hundred yards wide. In addition, there was a convergent path of wood and metal debris off to the northeast. Along this convergent path, a significant amount of debris was deposited in a tree line approximately a half mile to the northeast.||Additional damage was observed along the 1700 block of East Mitchell Road. An eyewitness saw the tornado a half mile to the southwest, and estimated the width as around 100 feet. He also observed large pieces of metal rotating in the tornado. At his property, an ornamental iron fence was bent down at a 45 degree angle due to a combination of winds associated with the tornado and debris from the location on North U.S. Route 27. The home also suffered some damage, including glass from a storm door blown in and broken glass from four windows. A circular item around three|feet in diameter and weighing two hundred pounds was lifted up, hit the house and then deposited about a couple hundred feet in a ditch behind the house. Numerous trees and large limbs across the property were also knocked down. A few of the trees reached diameters up to two feet.||Debris was evident in the field northeast of the East Mitchell Road residence. This debris was associated with the large metal building at the first location.||Additional damage was found further northeast at a residence on County Road 200 East. Several trees were downed, with one falling on the roof of a house and causing damage. This is the last known damage that occurred from this tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Wabash, White, Patoka, and Ohio Rivers rose back above flood stage late in the month. These river rises were in response to a series of nearly stationary fronts and outflow boundaries between the 16th and 24th. Numerous rounds of showers and storms occurred during this period. Rainfall totals during this time frame were between 3 and 6 inches. This round of river flooding was minor to moderate. The flooding continued into July at most locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding occurred on the Patoka River. Fields and woodlands in the flood plain of the Patoka River were inundated. At the river gage near Princeton, the river crested at 22.24 feet on the 24th. Flood stage is 18 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An organized line of severe thunderstorms formed over the central Plains states during the night of the 20th. This bowing line of storms progressed southeast across the St. Louis area during the midday hours, then down across the lower Ohio Valley during the afternoon. Isolated wind damage was reported in southwest Indiana. The atmosphere ahead of the storms became strongly unstable, with surface-based capes exceeding 4,000. Even though the region was under a broad 500 mb ridge, a moderately strong low-level feed of very moist and unstable air fueled the intense storms as they propagated southeastward.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were down. A flag pole was bent. Tree limbs were broken.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of lines of thunderstorms formed over southwest Indiana early in the afternoon. A few of these storms produced damaging wind. The storms occurred under a rather strong southwest wind flow ahead of a weak cold front over far western Missouri. A 500 mb shortwave trough over the western high Plains provided some additional support for the storms. The air mass was strongly unstable, with surface capes over 3000 j/kg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were down on county roads between Chrisney and Santa Claus, including County Roads 1100 North and 600 East.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms sank southward across southwest Indiana, producing isolated damaging wind gusts. The thunderstorm complex moved southward on the east side of a mid-level high centered over Missouri. A warm, moist, and unstable air mass provided a favorable environment for the storms to maintain their intensity into the lower Ohio Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong thunderstorm winds pushed southward across the Evansville metro area. Large tree limbs up to 8 inches in diameter were down on the east side of Evansville. A tree was down several miles west of Darmstadt. Several hundred utility customers were without power in the county. The peak wind gust at the Evansville airport was 55 mph, however the airport was outside the damage area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Wabash, White, Patoka, and Ohio Rivers rose back above flood stage late in the month. These river rises were in response to a series of nearly stationary fronts and outflow boundaries between the 16th and 24th. Numerous rounds of showers and storms occurred during this period. Rainfall totals during this time frame were between 3 and 6 inches. This round of river flooding was minor to moderate. The flooding continued into July at most locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wabash River crested at Mount Carmel at 27.26 feet on the 26th. This resulted in moderate flooding. Flood stage is 19 feet. There was considerable inundation of low-lying woodlands and fields. Several local roads were flooded, some by several feet of water. Extensive agricultural flooding was in progress in areas that are not protected by levees. This flood was very similar to the February flood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Wabash, White, Patoka, and Ohio Rivers rose back above flood stage late in the month. These river rises were in response to a series of nearly stationary fronts and outflow boundaries between the 16th and 24th. Numerous rounds of showers and storms occurred during this period. Rainfall totals during this time frame were between 3 and 6 inches. This round of river flooding was minor to moderate. The flooding continued into July at most locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding occurred on the White River. At the Hazleton river gage, the river crested at 26.30 feet on the 25th. Flood stage is 16 feet. Flood gates were installed at Hazleton. Water was nearly two feet deep in the west end of Hazleton, where water pumps were installed. People in cabins on the riverward side of the levee generally relocated. Many local roads were completely impassable. Oil fields were inaccessible. Agricultural impacts were significant. This flood was very similar to the February flood except for the impact on farmers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed on a stalled frontal boundary draped across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado briefly touched down damaging an older church building on North County Road 550 West. The roof collapsed within the building as the north wall was blown out. There was a large amount of splatter on the east facing wall. A piece of metal was located in a tree around forty yards to the west of the church. Debris from the church was lifted and scattered northward across the cemetery and up to 200 yards into a nearby field.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed on a stalled frontal boundary draped across the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado first touched down along South 37th Street, just to the south of Backmeyer Road, as indicated by swirl marks in an adjacent field. Two trees were snapped along South 37th Street.||The tornado then moved north-northeast, producing damage at the Brookdale Nursing Home on South A Street. Five to ten percent of the roof was removed. One tree, four inches in diameter, was knocked down and several smaller limbs from trees were also knocked down.||The most significant damage was observed at the Richmond Mall where several air handlers on the roof were removed and one was blown northeast onto U.S. 40. The parking lot was also full of debris and a large store on the southwest side of the mall had fifty percent of the glass blown in. On the east side of the mall, a brick wall seven feet high by thirty feet long was destroyed and some of the facade was starting to peel away from the roof. Around that same area, a garage door was blown in. Overall less than five percent of the roof of the mall was removed. On the inside of the mall approximately eight businesses experienced damage, primarily from windows blown out and merchandise blown around. Winds at the Richmond Mall were estimated to be around 85 mph, which is rated at the highest end for an EF0 tornado.||Some of the roof material from the mall landed in three to five businesses on the other side of US 40. Those businesses also received minor damage, with isolated windows being blown out and also some observed damage to signage and siding. A concrete table was moved several feet from its original position, and there was also minor small tree damage. In the service station at the intersection of US 40 and Hayes Arboretum Rd, an adult and a child were sitting in a car when the windows were blown in, causing minor injuries. They were taken to the hospital, treated and released.||Further northeast along Woodside Drive, a large hardwood tree fell to the southwest onto a house and also a tall metal television antenna fell the opposite direction onto the house. Extensive tree damage was also observed near the house, with several two foot diameter trees blown down. Debris from the signage of businesses was scattered throughout the property on Woodside Drive.||The property next door also experienced some damage, particularly with the garage being shifted and a tree limb also knocked down onto the house. Further down along Woodside Drive, several large tree limbs were knocked down. On Sycamore Drive, isolated two to three foot diameter trees were knocked down.||The last visible damage from this tornado appeared to be along Gravel Pit Road, where an additional one or two trees were knocked down.

A warm front brought severe storms, tornadoes, and heavy rain on June 15th. A significant tornado outbreak that was the largest since November 17, 2013 occurred during the evening of Saturday, June 15th. Seven tornadoes occurred in central Indiana with 3 additional tornadoes across portions of eastern Indiana.||This is what the environment showed. A weak shortwave trough was moving through central Indiana during the time of the severe weather.  This was a high shear, low CAPE environment initially which kept convection to a minimum until around 5 PM when just enough CAPE was there to rapidly increase storm coverage and severity.  LCL heights were around 750m at the time. The shear profile was ideal for creating numerous rotating thunderstorms.  With 0-1km Shear over 30 knots and Storm Relative Helicity in excess of 300 m^2/s^2, the low level environment was primed for tornadoes.  The 0-6km shear of 40-50 knots was sufficient for rotating supercells. The significant tornado parameter was not quite as impressive and was kept fairly low due to the low CAPE environment.  Had CAPE values been closer to 2000-3000 J/kg, a much more intense outbreak would have occurred.  In addition to the tornado threat, Precipitable Water values were in excess of 1.5 inches which also lead to significant flash flooding as the evening progressed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Major structural damage was reported at Hulsbosch Dairy Farm on County Road 700 West, north of County Road 700 South, due to damaging thunderstorm wind gusts. There was also a report of damage on nearby Shireman Dairy Farm and the Blauvent Farm. No specifics of what was damaged and to what degrees those structures were damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of thunderstorms across Wisconsin the early morning hours of May 16 moved south during the morning toward Indiana. Although the storms were initially though to weaken and dissipate, they were able to tap into instability to the west. The storms strengthened and increased in coverage, bringing the severe weather to the area during the afternoon and early evening. The storms moved along a gradient in instability, aided by a mid-level jet. Stronger shear and instability near the Illinois border lead to the development of a supercell thunderstorm, which produced 2.5 diameter hail. The storms produced mainly damaging winds, but the one aforementioned storm produced large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A power line was reported down due to damaging thunderstorm wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of thunderstorms across Wisconsin the early morning hours of May 16 moved south during the morning toward Indiana. Although the storms were initially though to weaken and dissipate, they were able to tap into instability to the west. The storms strengthened and increased in coverage, bringing the severe weather to the area during the afternoon and early evening. The storms moved along a gradient in instability, aided by a mid-level jet. Stronger shear and instability near the Illinois border lead to the development of a supercell thunderstorm, which produced 2.5 diameter hail. The storms produced mainly damaging winds, but the one aforementioned storm produced large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were reported down due to damaging thunderstorm wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front brought severe storms, tornadoes, and heavy rain on June 15th. A significant tornado outbreak that was the largest since November 17, 2013 occurred during the evening of Saturday, June 15th. Seven tornadoes occurred in central Indiana with 3 additional tornadoes across portions of eastern Indiana.||This is what the environment showed. A weak shortwave trough was moving through central Indiana during the time of the severe weather.  This was a high shear, low CAPE environment initially which kept convection to a minimum until around 5 PM when just enough CAPE was there to rapidly increase storm coverage and severity.  LCL heights were around 750m at the time. The shear profile was ideal for creating numerous rotating thunderstorms.  With 0-1km Shear over 30 knots and Storm Relative Helicity in excess of 300 m^2/s^2, the low level environment was primed for tornadoes.  The 0-6km shear of 40-50 knots was sufficient for rotating supercells. The significant tornado parameter was not quite as impressive and was kept fairly low due to the low CAPE environment.  Had CAPE values been closer to 2000-3000 J/kg, a much more intense outbreak would have occurred.  In addition to the tornado threat, Precipitable Water values were in excess of 1.5 inches which also lead to significant flash flooding as the evening progressed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The EF-2 rated tornado, with max wind speeds of 130 mph, began in rural countryside northeast of Ellettsville doing some tree damage before crossing State Road 46 and doing significant tree damage along Chafin Chapel Road. A 400 yard wide path of trees was completely shredded in spots. Additional widespread tree damage was found on Mount Tabor Road and West Cowden Road. Along West Cowden Road, several barns and outbuildings were destroyed as well as a loosely anchored garage completely swept off of its foundation. The tornado continued into rural forest before dissipating.

This allowed the storms to rotate and rapidly became severe, producing tornadoes, large hail, and damaging winds. The storms and tornadoes damaged homes, brought down many trees and power lines, and produced heavy rain as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front brought severe storms, tornadoes, and heavy rain on June 15th. A significant tornado outbreak that was the largest since November 17, 2013 occurred during the evening of Saturday, June 15th. Seven tornadoes occurred in central Indiana with 3 additional tornadoes across portions of eastern Indiana.||This is what the environment showed. A weak shortwave trough was moving through central Indiana during the time of the severe weather.  This was a high shear, low CAPE environment initially which kept convection to a minimum until around 5 PM when just enough CAPE was there to rapidly increase storm coverage and severity.  LCL heights were around 750m at the time. The shear profile was ideal for creating numerous rotating thunderstorms.  With 0-1km Shear over 30 knots and Storm Relative Helicity in excess of 300 m^2/s^2, the low level environment was primed for tornadoes.  The 0-6km shear of 40-50 knots was sufficient for rotating supercells. The significant tornado parameter was not quite as impressive and was kept fairly low due to the low CAPE environment.  Had CAPE values been closer to 2000-3000 J/kg, a much more intense outbreak would have occurred.  In addition to the tornado threat, Precipitable Water values were in excess of 1.5 inches which also lead to significant flash flooding as the evening progressed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A healthy, 10 foot tall tree was snapped due to damaging thunderstorm wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed near a warm front in an unstable environment, with plenty of low level shear/helicity, during the evening of Memorial Day, May 27th. This allowed the storms to rotate and rapidly became severe, producing tornadoes, large hail, and damaging winds. The storms and tornadoes damaged homes, brought down many trees and power lines, and produced heavy rain as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS Indianapolis found tornado damage 2 miles south of Middletown. The EF-1 rated tornado, with max wind speeds of 110 mph, brought down numerous branches and large tree trunks were snapped and lying in all directions. There was also a second hand report of someone who says they saw the tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of thunderstorms across Wisconsin the early morning hours of May 16 moved south during the morning toward Indiana. Although the storms were initially though to weaken and dissipate, they were able to tap into instability to the west. The storms strengthened and increased in coverage, bringing the severe weather to the area during the afternoon and early evening. The storms moved along a gradient in instability, aided by a mid-level jet. Stronger shear and instability near the Illinois border lead to the development of a supercell thunderstorm, which produced 2.5 diameter hail. The storms produced mainly damaging winds, but the one aforementioned storm produced large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree split in two near the base along New Haven Drive in Westfield due to damaging thunderstorm wind gusts. There may have been another tree next to it that blew over.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front brought severe storms, tornadoes, and heavy rain on June 15th. A significant tornado outbreak that was the largest since November 17, 2013 occurred during the evening of Saturday, June 15th. Seven tornadoes occurred in central Indiana with 3 additional tornadoes across portions of eastern Indiana.||This is what the environment showed. A weak shortwave trough was moving through central Indiana during the time of the severe weather.  This was a high shear, low CAPE environment initially which kept convection to a minimum until around 5 PM when just enough CAPE was there to rapidly increase storm coverage and severity.  LCL heights were around 750m at the time. The shear profile was ideal for creating numerous rotating thunderstorms.  With 0-1km Shear over 30 knots and Storm Relative Helicity in excess of 300 m^2/s^2, the low level environment was primed for tornadoes.  The 0-6km shear of 40-50 knots was sufficient for rotating supercells. The significant tornado parameter was not quite as impressive and was kept fairly low due to the low CAPE environment.  Had CAPE values been closer to 2000-3000 J/kg, a much more intense outbreak would have occurred.  In addition to the tornado threat, Precipitable Water values were in excess of 1.5 inches which also lead to significant flash flooding as the evening progressed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several houses had shingles blown off and numerous tree limbs were downed in Hymera due to damaging thunderstorm wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of thunderstorms across Wisconsin the early morning hours of May 16 moved south during the morning toward Indiana. Although the storms were initially though to weaken and dissipate, they were able to tap into instability to the west. The storms strengthened and increased in coverage, bringing the severe weather to the area during the afternoon and early evening. The storms moved along a gradient in instability, aided by a mid-level jet. Stronger shear and instability near the Illinois border lead to the development of a supercell thunderstorm, which produced 2.5 diameter hail. The storms produced mainly damaging winds, but the one aforementioned storm produced large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated thunderstorm wind gust of 60 mph was observed in this location. A gust of 53 mph was measured in this location as well.

A weak shortwave trough was moving through central Indiana during the time of the severe weather.  This was a high shear, low CAPE environment initially which kept convection to a minimum until around 5 PM when just enough CAPE was there to rapidly increase storm coverage and severity.  LCL heights were around 750m at the time. The shear profile was ideal for creating numerous rotating thunderstorms.  With 0-1km Shear over 30 knots and Storm Relative Helicity in excess of 300 m^2/s^2, the low level environment was primed for tornadoes.  The 0-6km shear of 40-50 knots was sufficient for rotating supercells. The significant tornado parameter was not quite as impressive and was kept fairly low due to the low CAPE environment.  Had CAPE values been closer to 2000-3000 J/kg, a much more intense outbreak would have occurred.  In addition to the tornado threat, Precipitable Water values were in excess of 1.5 inches which also lead to significant flash flooding as the evening progressed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The EF-1 rated tornado, with max wind speeds of 110 mph, began just southwest of downtown Freedom before moving into the town shearing numerous trees around town. The tornado continued to the northeast leaving a very clear path of sheared and uprooted trees in a path about 200 yards wide. The storm crossed the White River several times over its lifetime and followed the river line to south of Spencer. This is where a large building had its roof uplifted and walls collapsed. The building was part of a business that was set to open for the first time on Monday. The damage ended shortly after and the storm recycled into the next tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shower developed in northern Marshall County along the outflow from another shower farther southeast. This shower then collided with a trailing boundary of a Mesoscale Convective Vorticity Max that tracked across northeast Illinois into southern Lake Michigan. These boundary interactions within a very moist axis lended support for rapid intensification and enhanced low level rotation within this shower. This additional low level rotation allowed the shower to produce 2 brief tornadoes. Lightning was never observed with these showers with cloud tops remaining below 18,000 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Video footage indicated a separate wall cloud outside the parent |mesocyclone spawned a brief, thin tornado that touched down near the |intersection of Roosevelt Road and Hawthorne Trail. West facing barn |doors were slightly pushed in as the narrow circulation moved north |across the property with the only other damage noted occurring to |small branches on a nearby tree. The tornado moved north and impacted a outdoor screened structure, destroying it as well as removing siding and shingles from a detached garage. A resident was outside at the time the tornado struck and had to hold onto a beam as the wind hit. A dog was picked up a few feet off the ground and carried to the side of the home and placed down, unharmed. The tornado then lifted shortly after this, also supported by video footage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of thunderstorms across Wisconsin the early morning hours of May 16 moved south during the morning toward Indiana. Although the storms were initially though to weaken and dissipate, they were able to tap into instability to the west. The storms strengthened and increased in coverage, bringing the severe weather to the area during the afternoon and early evening. The storms moved along a gradient in instability, aided by a mid-level jet. Stronger shear and instability near the Illinois border lead to the development of a supercell thunderstorm, which produced 2.5 diameter hail. The storms produced mainly damaging winds, but the one aforementioned storm produced large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated thunderstorm wind gust of 60 mph was observed in this location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system brought two periods of heavy snow to central Indiana beginning Sunday afternoon and continuing through Monday night with a break during the day Monday.  Snowfall amounts were generally highest around Marion county and the surrounding counties with amounts ranging from 6 to 8 inches.  Lower amounts were seen across the rest of central Indiana with some minor icing across south central Indiana. Indiana State Police investigated 336 crashes with 79 causing personal injury. Indianapolis Metro Police Department responded to almost 200 incidents with over 75 slideoffs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall measurements ranged from 5.5 to 7.5 inches.  The highest amount was recorded near Strawtown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Central Indiana saw severe weather and flooding June 15th through the 17th. A warm front brought severe storms, tornadoes, and heavy rain on June 15th. The stalled front and upper waves brought more heavy rain and some severe storms on June 16th and 17th. The repeated rounds of heavy rain caused flooding across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding on Interstate 74 between mile markers 136 and 137 due to thunderstorm heavy rainfall.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Street flooding was occurring in Kokomo due to heavy rainfall.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small thunderstorm complex pushed southeast into Warren County during the evening of June 29th. Damaging wind occurred in this location as the storm moved across.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was blown onto the road at West Baltimore Hill Road and State Road 263 due to damaging thunderstorm wind gusts. Traffic had to be detoured around the tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low pressure system with influences from a remnant tropical system moved through central Indiana during the evening hours of October 26th.  This system brought strong gradient winds and widespread rain to the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was uprooted 3 miles west-southwest of Battle Ground due to strong gradient winds.  The area had experienced heavy rain in the hours leading up to the strong winds which allowed for weaker winds to uproot the tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous low in the mid levels of the atmosphere raced out of the central Rockies and became even stronger as it moved into the upper Midwest. As a result, the surface pressure gradient increased across the region. A cold front swept rapidly eastward during the early morning hours, causing winds to shift into the west and increase even further. Peak wind gusts were measured from 40 to 50 mph at most locations. The highest measured gust was 54 mph at the Evansville airport.

Frost formed as temperatures dropped into the lower and mid 30's. This was about ten days ahead of the date of the normal first freeze.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Central Indiana saw severe weather and flooding June 15th through the 17th. A warm front brought severe storms, tornadoes, and heavy rain on June 15th. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Many roads and lots were flooded in Bedford due to heavy rainfall. The intersection at M and 16th Streets was underwater and closed. All the rain fell within an hour.

Low-lying woods, fields, and a few county roads remained underwater. A trend toward somewhat drier weather allowed these rivers to fall below flood stage by mid month.
