Despite a persistent ridge in the upper levels of the atmosphere, scattered thunderstorms developed during the heat of the day. Weak southwest to west winds aloft brought a weak disturbance into the region. With little cap and strong surface-based instability, diurnal thunderstorms became locally very strong.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor structural damage occurred to farm outbuildings.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered to numerous thunderstorms again formed during the heat of the day and persisted well into the evening hours. Outflow boundaries from earlier storms served as the focus for new storms. A warm, moist air mass characterized by surface temperatures  mostly in the 80's and dew points around 70 resulted in moderate to strong instability. Given the buoyancy, water-loaded downdrafts resulted in isolated damaging winds. Weak wind shear resulted in unorganized, outflow dominant storms. The slow storm movements contributed to locally very heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An emergency manager reported a microburst north of the community of Reo. The damage path was about a mile and a half long. The damage started at the intersection of County Roads 450W and 400N. Many trees and five power poles were snapped. A peach crop was destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms moved across portions of northwest Indiana during the afternoon of July 21st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree damage was reported from the state line, through Morocco, east to Meridian Road and between County Roads 200 S and 500 S.  The bulk of the damage occurred in Morocco where numerous tree limbs, some several feet in diameter, were blown down.  A few trees fell onto houses and cars.  Utility poles and power lines were also blown down.  A few corn fields south of town were blown over.  A grove of trees was damaged near 200 S and Meridian Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of showers and thunderstorms pushed east across the across central Indiana during the afternoon hours of July the 29th. Stronger storms on the leading edge of this band from Indianapolis and just southward caused some damaging winds across Marion County and northwest Shelby County as the storms pushed northwest through the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was a report of roof damage to an apartment complex due to damaging thunderstorm wind gusts. The severity of the damage was unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of scattered thunderstorms developed from Frankfort to Muncie during the afternoon hours of July the 2nd. As these storms moved slowly eastward, one of the thunderstorms grew larger and more intense. This storm then pushed east-southeast through Muncie, producing damaging wind, as other storms to the east grew and merged with this storm.  Additional scattered thunderstorms developed later that afternoon and into the early evening from near Muncie to eastern Henry County. One of the stronger storms in Henry County produced some wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated 60 mph thunderstorm wind gust was observed in Muncie. Multiple trees and branches were reported down across the city, with at least one onto a home. Roof damage to a home was also reported. A few power poles and lines were downed as well.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated 65 mph thunderstorm wind gust was observed in this location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms developed in Iowa early in the morning of August 20, 2019, and continued moving southeast through the afternoon hours. Thunderstorms developed thanks to an upper level wave. The upper wave interacted with the unstable atmosphere to produce the severe thunderstorms. The storms brought damaging winds to central Indiana during the early to mid-afternoon time frame. Trees and power lines were downed across the area. The Indianapolis International Airport reported a 73 mph wind gust with the storms. Nearly 40,000 people were without power due to the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed across State Highway 450 near Brett Road due to thunderstorm wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms increased in coverage and intensity through the afternoon along and ahead of a cold front extending from southern Missouri to central Indiana. The storms organized into multi-cellular clusters, some of which produced damaging wind gusts.   Strong solar heating and surface dew points in the low to mid 70's resulted in moderately  strong instability. The storms were located on the fringes of stronger winds aloft, with around 25 to 30 knots of effective wind shear. However, the presence of moderately strong instability and copious moisture supported locally strong downburst winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple large tree limbs were down, shingles were torn from roofs, and a 16-foot utility trailer was moved two feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Combination of lake reinforcement/synoptic cool front aided in thunderstorm development. Overall storms were isolated (pulse storms) in nature but storm across Huntington/Wells/Adams did exhibit some supercell characteristics.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A healthy one foot diameter tree, as well as numerous eight to ten inch diameter branches were blown down across State Route 27 at High Street. A few shingles were also blown off a roof.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Combination of lake reinforcement/synoptic cool front aided in thunderstorm development. Overall storms were isolated (pulse storms) in nature but storm across Huntington/Wells/Adams did exhibit some supercell characteristics.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter measured a 63 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface high pressure was over the upper Mid-west while a cold front extended through  central Indiana to southwest through Missouri. In the mid and upper levels, northwest flow prevailed over southern Indiana and central Kentucky. Ahead of the cold front, a line of storms began with the heating of the day. This line traveled south as it intensified, becoming a Mesoscale Convective System (MCS) which produced large severe hail in southern Indiana and hail and damaging winds in central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The media relayed a report of one inch hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms sagged southward across southwest Indiana during the afternoon hours. Strong winds along the leading edge of this line produced isolated damage. The line of storms was initially well-organized as it moved into southern Indiana. Weak low to mid level wind shear was the main limiting factor for a more widespread severe wind event. Strong instability during the hot and humid afternoon hours resulted in the regeneration of intense storm clusters along the line. Although the line of storms became somewhat disorganized as it continued southward, low-level lapse rates were sufficient to support locally damaging winds with the strongest storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell on a structure in Patronville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On August 20th, southern Indiana and central Kentucky sat in an area of high pressure along with the rest of the southeastern United States. Upper level flow was zonal and stayed north of the area. During the day, temperatures climbed in to the 90s, so when an area of storms broke out in northern Iowa, it was able to bow and expand as it traveled southeast causing severe wind damage and in southern Indiana and central Kentucky. ||On August 22nd, the same air mass was in place when another Mesoscale Convective System (MCS) formed over southern Illinois and began to bow through southern Indiana. This caused tree damage and high winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dubois County Emergency Management reported a tree down at 15th and Leopold in Jasper. He also reported a transformer on fire at 9th and McArthur.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak shortwaves moved across the area within westerly flow during the afternoon and evening hours. Inverted trough and diffuse stationary boundary stretched from Northwest to Southeast across the area. SBCAPE around 2000 J/KG, poorly sheared,  in the 0-3 KM and 0-6 KM shear yet locally enhanced low level helicity (100m*2/s*2) existed near the boundary.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local media reported several trees and power lines were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms moved across parts of central Indiana late in the evening on August 12th and continued into the early hours of August 13th. One of the thunderstorms produced a brief tornado in Sullivan County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado began near the intersection of County Road 350 North and 1125 West and tracked northeast. There was damage to multiple corn fields as well as multiple areas of tree damage along the path. A swirling pattern was noted in the sheared and downed corn stalks on County Road 1050 West, north of County Road 350 North. The tornado damage came to an end a half a mile northeast of where County Road 350 North turns north and becomes County Road 1000 West.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of thunderstorms that developed over south central Illinois during the evening continued to enlarge as it tracked east across southwest Indiana. Isolated tree damage was associated with strong winds along the leading edge of this complex. The storms developed ahead of a minor 500 mb shortwave trough moving into northwest Missouri, embedded within a broad cyclonic flow over the Great Lakes region and upper Mississippi Valley. The atmosphere remained moist and unstable well into the evening. Elevated layer capes were around 2000 ahead of a weak cold front approaching the Interstate 70 corridor.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down in the Wadesville area.

The storms brought damaging winds to central Indiana during the early to mid-afternoon time frame. Trees and power lines were downed across the area. The Indianapolis International Airport reported a 73 mph wind gust with the storms. Nearly 40,000 people were without power due to the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large tree limbs downed due to thunderstorm wind gusts. A roof was blown off along with several trees uprooted or snapped near the location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low topped convective showers moved across northwest Indiana during the afternoon of August 26th producing one brief tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado occurred in a field outside of Kentland.  No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Remnants of Tropical Storm Olga pushed through the lower Ohio Valley and generated lines of showers. Some of these showers produced winds over 60 mph and sporadic wind damage in southern Indiana and central Kentucky. The showers were low-topped and the convection was weak. There was no trailing stratiform precipitation or low level cold pool behind the lines of convection. The lines of showers showed little bowing and no significant rotation. Strong synoptic winds associated with the tropical system���s remnants were mixed to the surface on the leading edge of clearing.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees across the county were brought down due to high winds, but power and property were not affected.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad, negative-tilt 500-mb trough was sweeping into the area. A 40-50 knot 850 mb jet was located over the eastern Great Lakes. At the surface, dew points were near 70 degrees in most locations, with a warm front located well into central Michigan. An outflow boundary was analyzed on the 15z WPC surface map located along the IN toll road near northwest IN. A cold front was analyzed from extreme NE IL to the Davenport, IA area. Moderate CAPE values were in place, but a poorly sheared environment existed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management officials reported a five foot diameter tree was blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large high-precipitation supercell moved into northwest portions of central Indiana during the afternoon hours of August 18th. This supercell then transitioned to compact bow echo as it moved eastward, impacting the heart of the Indy metro area. Multiple reports of trees and power lines downed, heavy rain, few large hail reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several small to medium size tree limbs were down and power was out in this location due to damaging thunderstorm wind gusts.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency Management reports multiple trees downed due to thunderstorm wind gusts. Power outages due to downed power lines were also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Combination of lake reinforcement/synoptic cool front aided in thunderstorm development. Overall storms were isolated (pulse storms) in nature but storm across Huntington/Wells/Adams did exhibit some supercell characteristics.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall late in November sent the White River above flood stage in early December. The flooding was minor, affecting mainly bottomland fields and woods.
Heavy rainfall late in November sent the White River above flood stage in early December. The flooding was minor, affecting mainly bottomland fields and woods.

A moist and unstable air mass sat over the Ohio River Valley. CAPE values exceeded 3000 J/kg, but shear was weak. This provided an environment for pulse type thunderstorms. As the heat of the day began to build, it became enough to kickoff area thunderstorms. Thunderstorms across southern Indiana caused several trees to fall, along with severe hail and heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were down along State Road 37.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between June 17-19, the Ohio Valley sat in a warm moist environment with a stationary front draped from northeast to southwest across Indiana and on through Texas. Little wind shear existed above southern Indiana. This provided several days where diurnal heating and shortwave forcing kicked off pulse storms with high rainfall rates. After a few days of this, the ground became more saturated and flooding became easier with each event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flood prone roads were closed due to high water at multiple locations across the county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines blew down in Grantsburg, Indiana.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Patchy dense fog cause some traffic issues and school delays. A motorcycle accident occurred in South Bend that was at least partially due to the foggy conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A car and motorcycle collided during dense fog, resulting in the death of a male on the motorcycle. No age was available and fog was considered a factor, but not necessarily the main cause.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface CAPES in excess of 3000 J/KG were in place ahead of a cold front and lake breeze boundary. Thunderstorms rapidly developed and became prolific hail producers. Portions of Steuben and DeKalb county seen multiple severe storms each producing hail from quarter to two inch diameter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported roof damage to a barn with several large trees blown down or snapped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On June 30th,  temperatures reached the low 90s with dewpoints neared 70. The area saw little inhibition. Thunderstorms started forming in areas covered by cumulus. Once these storms got going, outflow boundaries continued to propagate new cell formation and growth. The result was severe thunderstorm damage that affecting southern Indiana by knocking power and trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines were reported down along Jasper Dubois Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Remnants of overnight MCS moved across the area during the day with occasional strong storms during peak heating.  This activity left behind remnant outflows which focused late afternoon/evening storms across southern portions of the area. Additional storms developed along and just ahead of a cold front Tuesday evening into the early overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management officials reported numerous trees down across portions of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southward sinking boundary helped provide a focus for rapid thunderstorm development in a moderately unstable but poorly sheared environment. Some of the storms were able to reach severe levels across parts of the area.

Southward sinking boundary helped provide a focus for rapid thunderstorm development in a moderately unstable but poorly sheared environment. Some of the storms were able to reach severe levels across parts of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public shared photos on social media of a tree snapped at the base with some corn stalks blown over on State Route 114, West of North Manchester.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak, low amplitude southwest flow short wave kicked out of Missouri during the day. A 50 knot southwesterly low level jet developed Friday evening and resulted in continued warm/moist advection in pre-frontal environment.  Upper level jet dynamics also became more favorable by Friday evening in broad right entrance region of upper level jet.  PWATs again with this event were highly anomalous for late September, in the 1.75-2 inch range. While several rainfall reports in the two to four inch range were common across parts of the area, recent dry conditions help limit some of the flash flood threat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported trees and power lines down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms moved across northwest Indiana during the afternoon of May 27th producing large hail, wind damage and one tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down on the southeast side of Dyer and caused damage to trim, shingles, siding and fascia on several homes.  A chimney on one home was also damaged.  Multiple trees were snapped and many tree limbs were blown down.  A backyard shed was flipped and fencing was damaged.  Homes located between Willow and Scotty Lanes appeared to sustain the worst damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A few rivers remained above flood stage due to a persistent wet pattern through the winter and spring. The May rainfall total cracked the top five at Evansville, IN, where 9.15 inches of rain fell. Normally, 13 to 15 inches of rain falls between March and May. In 2019, however, between 15 and 20 inches of rain was common with some areas receiving between 20 and 25 inches. The Wabash, White, and Patoka Rivers experienced minor flooding from April into May. Fields and woodlands in the floodplain were inundated. Some county river roads were flooded. Access to hunting cabins was blocked. Oil fields in the floodplain were inaccessible.

An area of snow and gusty winds created difficult travel conditions during the morning hours of December 31st. Snow totals of 1 to 4 inches and wind gusts of 40 to 50 mph were common.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow and blowing snow, with temperatures falling into the 20s, led to ice and snow covered roads during the morning hours of December 31st. Total snow accumulations across the county generally ranged between 2 and 4 inches. This in combination with west winds gusting as high as 50 mph led to reduced visibilities and difficult travel during this time. There were numerous reports of accidents and slide-offs across the region.

Weak, low amplitude southwest flow short wave kicked out of Missouri during the day. A 50 knot southwesterly low level jet developed Friday evening and resulted in continued warm/moist advection in pre-frontal environment.  Upper level jet dynamics also became more favorable by Friday evening in broad right entrance region of upper level jet.  PWATs again with this event were highly anomalous for late September, in the 1.75-2 inch range. While several rainfall reports in the two to four inch range were common across parts of the area, recent dry conditions help limit some of the flash flood threat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported trees and power lines were blown down in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another round of storms occurred, producing heavy rain and localized flash flooding. The storms occurred in a strongly unstable air mass ahead of a dissipating cold front over northwest Missouri. A weakening 500 mb shortwave moved northeastward from the southern Plains, contributing to the development of the thunderstorms. Precipitable water values were nearly two inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A water rescue was conducted along County Road 550 E south of Francisco. West of Princeton, water was over County Road 100 N just west of County Road 500 W.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Horizontal flow in the upper levels stayed over the Great Lakes region with an embedded stationary front over northern Indiana. This provided little shear energy south of the front over southern Indiana and central Kentucky. With the heating of the day and outflow boundaries from area convection, isolated pulse thunderstorms caused hail in a few locations near the Ohio River in Indiana and Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media reported 1 inch hail near Charlestown, Indiana.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Split upper level flow rejoined and blew horizontally over southern Indiana and central Kentucky. High pressure was parked over the southeastern United States. This provided anticyclonic moist low level flow up the Mississippi and Ohio Valleys. On May 26th a front running west to east over northern Indiana slowly moved south towards the Ohio River kicking up severe storms in central Kentucky before retreating back north. On the 28th, the front was back in northern Indiana with a surface low. On May 29th, from the low pressure system, a bowing cold front pushed southeast towards the Ohio River kicking off heavy rainfall that caused localized flooding in southern Indiana and storms that produced severe hail in Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Deputies reported water was over State Road 56 west of Jasper near Ireland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal boundary, combined with a series of outflow boundaries, provided the necessary lift in an unstable and sheared environment to allow for strong to locally severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management officials reported a few utility poles were blown down in a few locations across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large thunderstorm complex tracked slowly east across southwest Indiana during the morning. These storms produced heavy rainfall and isolated flooding. A deep southwest wind flow through the depth of the atmosphere brought abundant moisture for storms. Precipitable water values were near 1.5 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Baseline Road was flooded west of Petersburg Road. Vehicles were stranded in the water. A trained spotter measured 1.2 inches of rain in 30 minutes.

A deep southwest wind flow through the depth of the atmosphere brought abundant moisture for storms. Precipitable water values were near 1.5 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of main roads were closed in the Fort Branch area. One bridge was flooded over. Several other roads were flooded but passable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of heavy rain fell on April 30th with another period of heavy rain during the early morning of May 1st, which caused flooding in many areas of Lake and Porter counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near Wheeler, 450 N was closed due to high water near 475 W and 400 N was closed due to high water west of 375 W.  Near South Haven, 200 W was closed due to flooding near Route 6.  Near Chesterton, 1300 N was closed due to flooding near the east arm of the Little Calumet River, west of 450 E and 1400 N was closed due to flooding west of Brummitt Road.  Near Valparaiso, an amusement center was flooded by Salt Creek near Route 30 and Route 2.  Numerous residential streets were flood in Portage.  East of Portage, Central Avenue and Samuelson Road were closed due to flooding from Robbins Ditch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A period of heavy rain fell on April 30th with another period of heavy rain during the early morning of May 1st, which caused flooding in many areas of Lake and Porter counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Near Crown Point, Clark Street between 91st and 101st Streets was flooded and 121st Avenue near Iowa Street was flooded.  In Hobart, Lakefront Park was flooded from Lake George.  Near Highland, standing water was reported in one lane in each direction of Interstate 80/94.  In Dyer, the intersection of 213 Street and Calumet Avenue was flooded.  Near Winfield, 97th Avenue was flooded between Randolph and Clay Streets.  North of Cedar Lake, 117th Avenue was flooded between Cline and Parrish Avenues.  South of Lowell, Belshaw Road was flooded at Cedar Creek.  In Schererville, multiple streets were flooded near Eagle Ridge Drive.  North of Sumava Resorts, some lanes of Route 41 were closed due to flooding from the Kankakee River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Remnants of overnight MCS moved across the area during the day with occasional strong storms during peak heating.  This activity left behind remnant outflows which focused late afternoon/evening storms across southern portions of the area. Additional storms developed along and just ahead of a cold front Tuesday evening into the early overnight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported downed trees and power line damage along railroad tracks near this location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave ejected northeast from the Central Plains through the Ohio Valley on Sunday evening, December 15th. Snowfall accumulations were generally 2 to 3 inches across portions of southeast and east central Indiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer northwest of Bright recorded 2.9 inches of snow. The observer west of Aurora measured 2.8 inches, and several social media reports of around 2.5 inches were noted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave ejected northeast from the Central Plains through the Ohio Valley on Sunday evening, December 15th. Snowfall accumulations were generally 2 to 3 inches across portions of southeast and east central Indiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter measured 2.8 inches of snow near Connersville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave ejected northeast from the Central Plains through the Ohio Valley on Sunday evening, December 15th. Snowfall accumulations were generally 2 to 3 inches across portions of southeast and east central Indiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Based on nearby reports, it is estimated that around 3 inches of snow affected the county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer located 4 miles south of Aurora measured 2 inches of snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer located southwest of Batesville measured 3.4 inches of snow. Another observer northeast of Osgood measured 3 inches of accumulation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave ejected northeast from the Central Plains through the Ohio Valley on Sunday evening, December 15th. Snowfall accumulations were generally 2 to 3 inches across portions of southeast and east central Indiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer in Liberty measured 3.2 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave ejected northeast from the Central Plains through the Ohio Valley on Sunday evening, December 15th. Snowfall accumulations were generally 2 to 3 inches across portions of southeast and east central Indiana.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The EMA in Richmond measured 3.2 inches of snow. The cooperative observer at the Richmond Water Works and a social media post from Centerville both measured 3 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rise on the Ohio River occurred during the second half of the month due to heavy rain across the central part of Kentucky. This produced a round of minor flooding in the Ohio River basin. The flooding was minor, affecting mainly dormant agricultural fields.

A rise on the Ohio River occurred during the second half of the month due to heavy rain across the central part of Kentucky. This produced a round of minor flooding in the Ohio River basin. The flooding was minor, affecting mainly dormant agricultural fields.
Wayne County received a few inches of snow on Monday evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public report from Richmond indicated that 2 inches of snow fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rapidly deepening low pressure system moved through the region in the pre-dawn hours on December 30, 2019, resulting in strong wind gusts in the 50-60 MPH range, with isolated gusts of 70 MPH.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An awning to a gas station was blown down in Aurora. Trees were blocking a road in Greendale.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong winds gusting between 45 mph and 55 mph during the morning of October 21st blew down numerous large tree limbs in Highland.  One large tree was blown down onto a home in East Chicago.

A negatively tilted trough, combined with a 130 knot jet streak forced the rapid development and intensification of seasonably deep low pressure. The low tracked across  northeastern Illinois and then into central Lake Michigan. While some stronger wind gusts were noted along a cold front, pockets of damaging winds occurred behind the front with 50 to 60 mph wind gusts common, resulting in power outages, trees and power lines down and some minor structure damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees, tree limbs, power lines and poles were all reported down in spots across the county. A mesonet site southeast of Domestic recorded a 64 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the early morning hours of November 19th, patchy dense fog formed across portions of east central Indiana. This persisted through around 9 AM and was attributed to a fatal accident on Interstate 70 east of Indianapolis.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Just before 8 AM EST, Indiana State Police was called to the 125-mile marker on I-70 East, when a pickup truck crashed into the back of a semi. Police said the thick fog resulting in limited visibility is thought to have played a factor in the fatal crash. Unsafe speed was also a factor in the crash, according to police.

While some stronger wind gusts were noted along a cold front, pockets of damaging winds occurred behind the front with 50 to 60 mph wind gusts common, resulting in power outages, trees and power lines down and some minor structure damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees, tree limbs, power lines and poles were all reported down in spots across the county. A 60 mph wind gust was recorded along the lakeshore area near Pottawattomie Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong northerly winds produced high waves which combined with high lake levels produced lake shore flooding.  In Lake County, Lakefront Park in Whiting was flooded.  The cold temperatures allowed freezing spray from the high waves to coat many benches, trees and poles near the shore in several inches of ice.  In Porter County, beach erosion and flooding occurred at Portage Lakefront and Riverwalk Pavilion.  The high water level breached a sand dune separating the beach from a rare geological wetland feature near the pavilion.

Rain changed to snow behind an arctic cold front on November 11th. This then transitioned to heavy lake effect showers late on November 11th into the morning hours of November 12th across portions of far north-central Indiana. Total snow accumulations generally ranged between 3 and 6 inches, though locally heavier amounts were common in areas that received heavy lake effect snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain changed over to widespread moderate snow during the early afternoon hours of November 11th. The snow then continued through the remainder of the afternoon before tapering off from west to east in the evening. Total snow accumulations generally ranged between 3 and 4 inches. Roads quickly became snow covered with a few accidents and slide-offs reported across the region, along with school delays and closings the following day.

Very strong winds developed during the early morning hours and continued through mid afternoon on November 27th.  A wind gust to 65 mph was measured at Valparaiso Airport.

A strong upper level system brought strong gradient winds to much of Indiana with the strongest winds generally along and north of Interstate 70. These strong winds caused widespread power outages, damaged buildings, and caused significant tree damage. Over 200 crews were dispatched to return power to Marion County alone. At the peak of the event, at least 70,416 homes were without power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 58 mph non-thunderstorm wind gust was measured at the KLAF ASOS.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rain changed over to widespread moderate snow during the early afternoon hours of November 11th. The snow then continued through the remainder of the afternoon before tapering off from west to east in the evening. Total snow accumulations generally ranged between 3 and 8 inches, heaviest across far western portions of the county where a heavy lake effect snow band settled in for a time. Snowfall rates of 1-2 inches per hour and whiteout conditions were common with this heavy snow band, which made for very difficult travel. Roads being snow covered, and reduced visibilities, led to a few accidents and slide-offs, along with numerous school delays and closings across the region.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A round of significant flooding occurred on the last day of November as a warm front lifted north, bringing warm moist air and providing a focus for showers and thunderstorms. Several rounds of moderate to heavy rain moved across the area. The earlier rounds of rain primed the ground for flooding. Rainfall totals of three to four inches led to flash flooding in the region. Some of the stronger thunderstorms produced hail up to the size of dimes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A shopping mall was struck by lightning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A rare early season winter weather event occurred on Veterans Day, with snowfall amounts ranging from 1 to 2 inches. Locally higher amounts near 3 inches occurred along the Wabash River. A robust surge of arctic air blasted into the region during the afternoon hours. The colder temperatures caused the rain to change to a brief period of sleet or freezing rain, before quickly transitioning to snow. The changeover occurred during the afternoon and early evening hours. Flash freeze conditions were observed on area roadways as gusty northwest winds accompanied the temperature drop into the 20s. Although road surface temperatures were well above freezing at the start, travel impacts quickly occurred as sunset approached. Many area roads became slick and hazardous, which resulted in numerous accidents and slide offs. The poor road conditions continued into the next morning due to very cold temperatures down into the teens, which caused icy patches on any untreated roads. A record low of 13 degrees was established in Evansville. Wind chills were down around 0, and even below zero in several locations. The wintry weather was caused by a combination of systems. A strong cold front sagged slowly southeast across the lower Ohio Valley as an upper-level disturbance swung southeast from the Canadian prairie provinces.
An early season snowfall was the result of the passage of a strong cold front, affecting the region Monday evening before ending overnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The observer in Alpine measured 4 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level system brought strong gradient winds to much of Indiana with the strongest winds generally along and north of Interstate 70. These strong winds caused widespread power outages, damaged buildings, and caused significant tree damage. Over 200 crews were dispatched to return power to Marion County alone. At the peak of the event, at least 70,416 homes were without power.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous tree limbs were knocked down across the county due to non-thunderstorm wind gusts.  At least 812 homes were without power at one point during the event.

Strong northerly winds as high as 50 mph produced waves as high as 14 feet along the Lake Michigan shore of northwest Indiana.  These high waves combined with high lake levels produced flooding, erosion and damage.  In Lake County, Lakefront Park in Whiting Indiana was flooded.  In Porter County, beach erosion and flooding occurred at Portage Lakefront and Riverwalk Pavilion.  A small boat was broke up against rocks near the Ogden Dunes shore.

June 21 started a period of active weather for southern Indiana and central Kentucky. ||On June 21, A large Mesoscale Convective System (MCS) formed over Missouri early in the day. By afternoon, a line of severe storms had made its way into southern Indiana, doing isolated damage. This line continued south and southeast into Kentucky. ||On June 23, in much of the same way, large MCSs that originated in Kansas and Missouri continued traveling south and east while expanding. These systems took advantage of afternoon heating that fueled stronger lines and expanded stretching over multiple states. The result was severe damage in a few southern Indiana counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Emergency Manager reported a tree down across Old State Road 64 near Huntingburg. There was another report of a tree down across Division Road west of Jasper.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree limb broke and fell onto power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system brought two periods of heavy snow to central Indiana beginning Sunday afternoon and continuing through Monday night with a break during the day Monday.  Snowfall amounts were generally highest around Marion county and the surrounding counties with amounts ranging from 6 to 8 inches.  Lower amounts were seen across the rest of central Indiana with some minor icing across south central Indiana. Indiana State Police investigated 336 crashes with 79 causing personal injury. Indianapolis Metro Police Department responded to almost 200 incidents with over 75 slideoffs.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts ranged from 3.7 to 5.4 inches across the count with the highest across the northeastern portions of the county. Impacts to travel were significant with evening rush hour call load to 911 dispatch 4 to 6 times the average. There were a total of 60 accidents with 6 injuries that were determined to be related to the snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the evening hours along a warm front lifting slowly north across the region.
An upper level disturbance produced occasional showers and embedded thunderstorms during the morning hours, leading to localized flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High water was reported on several roads across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of thunderstorms moved across northwest Indiana during the afternoon and evening of September 27th producing torrential rain and flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flood waters several feet deep were reported near Lake Street and Greenwood Avenue.  Part of a residential street and sidewalk were washed out near a retention pond.  Storm total rainfall of 5.44 inches was measured in Crown Point.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of thunderstorms moved across northwest Indiana during the afternoon and evening of September 27th producing torrential rain and flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flood waters six inches deep were reported near 169th Street and Cline Avenue.  Storm total rainfall of 3.43 inches was measured one mile south southwest of Hammond.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A MCS tracked east-northeast from east-central MO/central IL Wednesday evening (May 22) through the IWX CWA early Thursday morning (May 23). Widespread wind gusts of 50-60 mph, with embedded channeled microburst gusts in excess of 70 mph, occurred with associated bowing segments.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter measured a 70 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although the area was in the wake of a MCV, a west to east instability gradient was in place across northern Indiana and Ohio during the late evening of May 16 and early overnight hours of May 17. A strengthening low level jet, MUCAPES of at least 2000 J/KG along with a EML intruding that allowed mid level lapse rates to approach 7 C/km. Sufficent shear exists to allow several supercell type storms to develop and maintain large hail cores, with hail greater than two inches falling in a few locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public shared a picture on twitter of a large barn with roughly one quarter of its roof blown off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front surged north across the region, allowing for a moderately unstable, highly sheared environment to exist. Thunderstorms developed in Illinois and moved into northern Indiana, producing damaging winds and several tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tornado started nearly one and a half miles north of Macy. Lots of tree damage and roofing material removed. Satellite tornado destroyed a grain silo that was tossed into a tree line about quarter mile southeast and completely destroyed a barn building as well. Tornado intensified and broadened its width as it carried ENE and completely leveled a two story house with debris scattered downstream across a field and across CR 200 W. It then passed through a heavily wooded area snapping or uprooting most trees before crossing CR 100 W and then completely destroying a single story brick ranch home. Adjacent machine shed was heavily damaged here as well and included a pickup truck that was picked up and tossed northeast of the house. Extensive tree damage continues ENE from here through a large grove of trees. Tornado continued northeast crossing West Pleasant Hill Road where two high voltage transmission towers were destroyed as well as extensive damage to structures in the area. Tornado continued northeast crossing north Meridian Road and heavily damaging two large farm properties with extensive structural damage noted. The tornado straddled the Miami/Fulton county line before crossing into Fulton county somewhere along E County Road 1600 N between N County Roads 100 E and 175 E. The maximum intensity of the tornado in Miami county was EF3 with maximum winds of around 140 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front surged north across the region, allowing for a moderately unstable, highly sheared environment to exist. Thunderstorms developed in Illinois and moved into northern Indiana, producing damaging winds and several tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Miami County emergency management provided a video of the developing tornado northeast of Bunker Hill. The NWS received reports of damage the tornado path from emergency management. Most of the damage occurred in the vicinity of E County Road 550 South. Near the beginning of the tornado path, shingle and siding damage occurred at a residence with a fence also blown down. A TV tower was also bent halfway up. Additional shingle and siding damage occurred at a residence as the tornado crossed E County Road 550 South. Additional tree damage included tree trunks snapped near the end of the tornado path just to the north of E County Road 550 South.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front surged north across the region, allowing for a moderately unstable, highly sheared environment to exist. Thunderstorms developed in Illinois and moved into northern Indiana, producing damaging winds and several tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This is a continuation of a tornado that crossed County Road 1600 N between County Roads 100 and 175 East in Miami county and into Fulton County. Tornado continued off to the ENE weakening as it crossed 1600 N and 100 E in Fulton county. Tornado then turned northeastward as it traveled just north of the Miami/Fulton county line causing sporadic damage to several farm buildings. Tornado continued to slowly |weaken in intensity as it crossed SR 114.  As the tornado crossed CR 700 W it re-intensified for a time crossing SR 15 south of CR 1400 N with extensive damage to a hog confinement facility west of SR 15 and significant damage to a farm property |east of SR 15. From here the tornado rapidly weakened eastward and lifted near CR 400 W. The maximum intensity of the tornado in Fulton county was EF1.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although the area was in the wake of a MCV, a west to east instability gradient was in place across northern Indiana and Ohio during the late evening of May 16 and early overnight hours of May 17. A strengthening low level jet, MUCAPES of at least 2000 J/KG along with a EML intruding that allowed mid level lapse rates to approach 7 C/km. Sufficent shear exists to allow several supercell type storms to develop and maintain large hail cores, with hail greater than two inches falling in a few locations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Although the area was in the wake of a MCV, a west to east instability gradient was in place across northern Indiana and Ohio during the late evening of May 16 and early overnight hours of May 17. A strengthening low level jet, MUCAPES of at least 2000 J/KG along with a EML intruding that allowed mid level lapse rates to approach 7 C/km. Sufficent shear exists to allow several supercell type storms to develop and maintain large hail cores, with hail greater than two inches falling in a few locations.

A warm front surged north across the region, allowing for a moderately unstable, highly sheared environment to exist. Thunderstorms developed in Illinois and moved into northern Indiana, producing damaging winds and several tornadoes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The same supercell that produced a tornado in Grant |County moved east and underwent several storm mergers as it moved |over eastern Grant County. The merger caused the thunderstorm to |shift southeastwards with time before mirroring the Blackford and |Wells County lines. This thunderstorm produced a tornado with |multiple vortices, and had a width of approximately 1200 yards at |its widest. This tornado tracked from north of Roll, IN to north of |Montpelier, IN to near Nottingham, IN. The tornado lifted just east |of Highway 1. There were two significant injuries reported at a |dairy farm where EF-3 damage occurred.  EF-3 damage was observed in two locations. The first was at a dairy |farm on county road 700 West north of the county line. A well built |structure sustained major damage consistent with winds of 165 mph. |Numerous other buildings at this location sustained significant |damage and several cows also died. Further east, about 1 mile east |of county road 500 west, a large power transmission tower was |toppled in the middle of a field. Winds were estimated at 140 mph at |this location. Numerous other buildings sustained EF-0 to EF-1 |damage and some EF-2 damage was also observed. Many trees were |topped or uprooted all along the path of this tornado.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a prolonged period of heavy rain in April (rainfall amounts were 150 to 200 percent above normal) widespread flooding was reported throughout much of Northern and Central Indiana. Flood waters had started to recede in the early part of May but many low lying areas and tributaries were high and standing water was reported in many flood prone areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State police say 22-year-old Anthonitte Carter of Indianapolis was driving east on CR 925 South east of SR 13 in Wabash County when she passed a high water sign and drove into floodwaters. After the vehicle entered the water, it was carried to the north side of the road and became completely submerged, police said.||The driver was able to get out of her Chevrolet Impala and attempted to rescue her son, 2-year-old Eric Long, but was not successful in freeing him.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A MCS tracked east-northeast from east-central MO/central IL Wednesday evening (May 22) through the IWX CWA early Thursday morning (May 23). Widespread wind gusts of 50-60 mph, with embedded channeled microburst gusts in excess of 70 mph, occurred with associated bowing segments.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Damage pictures and an eyewitness report indicate a brief, small torando formed just west of a large farm in northeast Grant county. The tornado moved above a tens of a mile in an east northeast direction, substantially damaging a newer farm building. The roofing material of this barn was lifted and thrown east over the top of two other barns which only sustained minor damage. The roof trusses were flipped upside down, mainly landing inside the building. Additional roofing and siding material from the building was hurled east into and over grain silos and barns on the east side of the property. This debris was strewn up to approximately severe tenths of a mile east across an open field. Damage was consistent with an EF-1 tornado with wind speeds to 105 mph.
